main_paper.raw: {"methods":["Model Predictive Control (MPC) based nonstationary policy with rolling computational horizon τ","Finite-horizon linear programming (LP) update at each time-slot (LP-update algorithm)","Use of dissipativity framework for analysis"],"problems":["Restless Markovian Bandit (RMAB) problem: discrete time, infinite horizon, average reward setting with resource constraints","Designing practical and theoretically sound policies for RMABs that are easy to implement and have provable performance guarantees under weak assumptions"],"datasets":[],"metrics":["Suboptimality gap (difference from optimal average reward)","Rate of convergence (as a function of number of arms N)","Computational efficiency (practical performance, e.g., runtime)"],"results":[{"metric":"Suboptimality gap (general case)","value":"O(1/√N)"},{"metric":"Suboptimality gap (under local-stability condition)","value":"exp(-Ω(N))"}],"novelty_claims":["First to prove that a simple model predictive control (MPC) policy with finite planning horizon is asymptotically optimal for RMABs under very weak and easy to verify assumptions, using the dissipativity framework.","Provides theoretical guarantees and practical performance without requiring hard-to-verify assumptions like indexability or UGAP.","Introduces the use of dissipativity from dynamic control theory to analyze RMAB policies, which is a new technical approach in this context.","Demonstrates that the proposed MPC algorithm is easy to implement and outperforms state-of-the-art algorithms in benchmarks.","Proof methodology and algorithm can be generalized to more general constrained MDP settings."]}

methods: Model Predictive Control (MPC) based nonstationary policy with rolling computational horizon τ
Finite-horizon linear programming (LP) update at each time-slot (LP-update algorithm)
Use of dissipativity framework for analysis
problems: Restless Markovian Bandit (RMAB) problem: discrete time, infinite horizon, average reward setting with resource constraints
Designing practical and theoretically sound policies for RMABs that are easy to implement and have provable performance guarantees under weak assumptions
metrics: Suboptimality gap (difference from optimal average reward)
Rate of convergence (as a function of number of arms N)
novelty_claims: First to prove that a simple model predictive control (MPC) policy with finite planning horizon is asymptotically optimal for RMABs under very weak and easy to verify assumptions, using the dissipativity framework.
Provides theoretical guarantees and practical performance without requiring hard-to-verify assumptions like indexability or UGAP.
Introduces the use of dissipativity from dynamic control theory to analyze RMAB policies, which is a new technical approach in this context.
Demonstrates that the proposed MPC algorithm is easy to implement and outperforms state-of-the-art algorithms in benchmarks.
Proof methodology and algorithm can be generalized to more general constrained MDP settings.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Mean-Field Planning Algorithm (MFP) for RMABs","Construction of counterexamples to demonstrate sub-optimality of Whittle index policies in indexable RMABs","Non-asymptotic analysis of MFP with high-probability bounds and matching lower bounds"],"problems":["Planning in restless multi-armed bandits (RMABs) with multiple actions","Sub-optimality of Whittle index policies even in indexable RMABs","Obtaining near-optimal policies for RMABs without restrictive structural assumptions","Efficient and reliable planning for large-scale RMABs"],"datasets":[],"metrics":["Expected reward","Sub-optimality gap (difference from optimal policy)","High-probability performance bounds"],"results":[{"metric":"Expected reward (MFP vs. Whittle index policy)","value":"MFP matches or outperforms Whittle index policy in all experiments with large number of agents"},{"metric":"Sub-optimality gap of Whittle index policy in constructed examples","value":"Whittle index policy can be arbitrarily bad compared to optimal policy, even in indexable RMABs"},{"metric":"Non-asymptotic convergence rate of MFP","value":"Tight polynomial dependence on problem parameters; high-probability bounds provided"}],"novelty_claims":["Demonstration that Whittle index policies can be arbitrarily sub-optimal even in indexable RMABs, contrary to common belief","Proposal of a mean-field planning algorithm (MFP) that is hyper-parameter free and works under minimal assumptions","Provision of improved, non-asymptotic analysis for MFP with tighter polynomial dependence on problem parameters and high-probability performance guarantees","Matching sub-optimality lower bounds for MFP, demonstrating tightness of the analysis","Extensive experimental validation showing MFP matches or outperforms state-of-the-art index-based policies"]}

methods: Mean-Field Planning Algorithm (MFP) for RMABs
Construction of counterexamples to demonstrate sub-optimality of Whittle index policies in indexable RMABs
Non-asymptotic analysis of MFP with high-probability bounds and matching lower bounds
problems: Planning in restless multi-armed bandits (RMABs) with multiple actions
Sub-optimality of Whittle index policies even in indexable RMABs
Obtaining near-optimal policies for RMABs without restrictive structural assumptions
Efficient and reliable planning for large-scale RMABs

results.metric: Expected reward (MFP vs. Whittle index policy)
results.value: MFP matches or outperforms Whittle index policy in all experiments with large number of agents

results.metric: Sub-optimality gap of Whittle index policy in constructed examples
results.value: Whittle index policy can be arbitrarily bad compared to optimal policy, even in indexable RMABs
Tight polynomial dependence on problem parameters; high-probability bounds provided

novelty_claims: Demonstration that Whittle index policies can be arbitrarily sub-optimal even in indexable RMABs, contrary to common belief
Proposal of a mean-field planning algorithm (MFP) that is hyper-parameter free and works under minimal assumptions
Provision of improved, non-asymptotic analysis for MFP with tighter polynomial dependence on problem parameters and high-probability performance guarantees
Matching sub-optimality lower bounds for MFP, demonstrating tightness of the analysis
Extensive experimental validation showing MFP matches or outperforms state-of-the-art index-based policies

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Relaxing the entire bandit MDP as a deterministic optimal-control problem via the certainty equivalence control principle","'Align and steer' strategy for constructing asymptotically optimal policies","Integration of model predictive control within the 'align and steer' strategy"],"problems":["Undiscounted infinite-horizon discrete-time restless N-armed bandit problem","Designing asymptotically optimal policies for restless bandits with minimal assumptions","Overcoming the limitations of the unichain condition in restless bandit models"],"datasets":[],"metrics":[],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["Proposes relaxing the entire bandit MDP as an optimal-control problem, rather than the conventional single-armed relaxation.","Demonstrates that reachability of an optimal stationary state is a sufficient condition for asymptotic optimality, removing the need for the unichain assumption.","Introduces the 'align and steer' strategy for constructing asymptotically optimal policies under the reachability assumption.","Shows that the proposed approach applies to multichain models, broadening the applicability beyond previous work."]}

methods: Relaxing the entire bandit MDP as a deterministic optimal-control problem via the certainty equivalence control principle
'Align and steer' strategy for constructing asymptotically optimal policies
Integration of model predictive control within the 'align and steer' strategy
problems: Undiscounted infinite-horizon discrete-time restless N-armed bandit problem
Designing asymptotically optimal policies for restless bandits with minimal assumptions
Overcoming the limitations of the unichain condition in restless bandit models
novelty_claims: Proposes relaxing the entire bandit MDP as an optimal-control problem, rather than the conventional single-armed relaxation.
Demonstrates that reachability of an optimal stationary state is a sufficient condition for asymptotic optimality, removing the need for the unichain assumption.
Introduces the 'align and steer' strategy for constructing asymptotically optimal policies under the reachability assumption.
Shows that the proposed approach applies to multichain models, broadening the applicability beyond previous work.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Fluid-balance policies: a novel class of non-stationary, computationally scalable policies for infinite-horizon restless bandits, using a sequence of Lagrange multipliers (one per time period up to a finite truncation point) instead of a single global Lagrange multiplier as in the Whittle index.","Linear programming relaxation: analysis and computation based on a finite linear program formed by truncating the infinite-horizon problem at O(log N) periods."],"problems":["Scalable computation of near-optimal policies for infinite-horizon discounted restless bandits with many arms, where a fixed proportion of arms may be pulled in each period and arms share a finite state space.","Overcoming the computational intractability (curse of dimensionality) of computing average-case-optimal policies for large N.","Eliminating the need for hard-to-verify indexability and stability conditions required by the Whittle index for asymptotic optimality."],"datasets":["Two numerical experiments (details not specified in the provided content)"],"metrics":["Optimality gap (difference in expected performance between the proposed policy and the optimal policy)","Expected total discounted reward"],"results":[{"metric":"Optimality gap","value":"O(sqrt(N)) (i.e., the gap between the proposed policy and the optimal policy grows at most as the square root of the number of arms)"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes fluid-balance policies, a new class of non-stationary policies for infinite-horizon restless bandits that are computationally scalable and achieve O(sqrt(N)) optimality gap.","Fluid-balance policies do not require the indexability condition to be well-defined, unlike the Whittle index.","The O(sqrt(N)) optimality gap bound for fluid-balance policies holds universally, without the need for hard-to-verify sufficient conditions required by previous approaches.","Demonstrates that a time-inhomogeneous (non-stationary) approach, previously used only for finite-horizon problems, can be successfully generalized to the infinite-horizon setting.","Provides theoretical and empirical evidence that fluid-balance policies achieve state-of-the-art performance on specific problems."]}

methods: Fluid-balance policies: a novel class of non-stationary, computationally scalable policies for infinite-horizon restless bandits, using a sequence of Lagrange multipliers (one per time period up to a finite truncation point) instead of a single global Lagrange multiplier as in the Whittle index.
Linear programming relaxation: analysis and computation based on a finite linear program formed by truncating the infinite-horizon problem at O(log N) periods.
problems: Scalable computation of near-optimal policies for infinite-horizon discounted restless bandits with many arms, where a fixed proportion of arms may be pulled in each period and arms share a finite state space.
Overcoming the computational intractability (curse of dimensionality) of computing average-case-optimal policies for large N.
Eliminating the need for hard-to-verify indexability and stability conditions required by the Whittle index for asymptotic optimality.
datasets: Two numerical experiments (details not specified in the provided content)
metrics: Optimality gap (difference in expected performance between the proposed policy and the optimal policy)

results.value: O(sqrt(N)) (i.e., the gap between the proposed policy and the optimal policy grows at most as the square root of the number of arms)

novelty_claims: Proposes fluid-balance policies, a new class of non-stationary policies for infinite-horizon restless bandits that are computationally scalable and achieve O(sqrt(N)) optimality gap.
Fluid-balance policies do not require the indexability condition to be well-defined, unlike the Whittle index.
The O(sqrt(N)) optimality gap bound for fluid-balance policies holds universally, without the need for hard-to-verify sufficient conditions required by previous approaches.
Demonstrates that a time-inhomogeneous (non-stationary) approach, previously used only for finite-horizon problems, can be successfully generalized to the infinite-horizon setting.
Provides theoretical and empirical evidence that fluid-balance policies achieve state-of-the-art performance on specific problems.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Follow-the-Virtual-Advice (FTVA) framework for discrete-time restless bandits","FTVA-CT (continuous-time variant of FTVA) for continuous-time restless bandits","Simulation-based reduction from single-armed to N-armed policy","LP relaxation and simulated system for constraint satisfaction"],"problems":["Efficient computation of asymptotically optimal policies for infinite-horizon restless bandit problems with average reward criterion as the number of arms N grows large","Achieving asymptotic optimality without relying on the uniform global attractor property (UGAP)","Establishing non-trivial convergence rates for the optimality gap without UGAP or non-singularity/non-degeneracy assumptions"],"datasets":[],"metrics":["Optimality gap (difference between average reward per arm of the policy and that of the optimal policy)"],"results":[{"metric":"Optimality gap (discrete-time, FTVA(π*))","value":"O(1/√N) under Synchronization Assumption (SA) and unichain"},{"metric":"Optimality gap (continuous-time, FTVA-CT(π*))","value":"O(1/√N) under unichain assumption only"}],"novelty_claims":["First asymptotic optimality results for average-reward restless bandits that do not require the uniform global attractor property (UGAP) in both discrete-time (under SA) and continuous-time (no extra assumptions) settings.","Propose a general, simulation-based framework (FTVA/FTVA-CT) that converts any single-armed policy into a policy for the N-armed problem with vanishing performance loss.","Achieve O(1/√N) optimality gap without requiring non-singularity or non-degeneracy assumptions.","FTVA framework can be efficiently implemented with linear-in-N computational cost and allows for distributed implementation over arms.","Demonstrate existence of problem instances where prior policies (Whittle Index, LP-Priority) fail but FTVA achieves asymptotic optimality."]}

methods: Follow-the-Virtual-Advice (FTVA) framework for discrete-time restless bandits
FTVA-CT (continuous-time variant of FTVA) for continuous-time restless bandits
Simulation-based reduction from single-armed to N-armed policy
LP relaxation and simulated system for constraint satisfaction
problems: Efficient computation of asymptotically optimal policies for infinite-horizon restless bandit problems with average reward criterion as the number of arms N grows large
Achieving asymptotic optimality without relying on the uniform global attractor property (UGAP)
Establishing non-trivial convergence rates for the optimality gap without UGAP or non-singularity/non-degeneracy assumptions
metrics: Optimality gap (difference between average reward per arm of the policy and that of the optimal policy)

results.value: O(1/√N) under Synchronization Assumption (SA) and unichain

novelty_claims: First asymptotic optimality results for average-reward restless bandits that do not require the uniform global attractor property (UGAP) in both discrete-time (under SA) and continuous-time (no extra assumptions) settings.
Propose a general, simulation-based framework (FTVA/FTVA-CT) that converts any single-armed policy into a policy for the N-armed problem with vanishing performance loss.
Achieve O(1/√N) optimality gap without requiring non-singularity or non-degeneracy assumptions.
FTVA framework can be efficiently implemented with linear-in-N computational cost and allows for distributed implementation over arms.
Demonstrate existence of problem instances where prior policies (Whittle Index, LP-Priority) fail but FTVA achieves asymptotic optimality.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Two-set policy: maintains two dynamic subsets of arms, applying Optimal Local Control to one and driving the other towards optimal state distribution before merging","Novel multivariate Lyapunov function technique for analysis"],"problems":["Achieving exponential asymptotic optimality in infinite-horizon average-reward restless bandit problems without the global attractor assumption (UGAP)","Understanding the fundamental mechanisms and assumptions required for exponential asymptotic optimality in restless bandits"],"datasets":[],"metrics":["Optimality gap (difference between average reward per arm under the policy and the optimal policy)"],"results":[{"metric":"Optimality gap","value":"O(exp(-C N)) under aperiodic-unichain, non-degeneracy, and local stability assumptions"},{"metric":"Lower bound on optimality gap for unstable RBs","value":"Ω(1/sqrt(N))"}],"novelty_claims":["First policy (two-set policy) to achieve exponential asymptotic optimality (O(exp(-C N)) optimality gap) in average-reward restless bandits without requiring the global attractor assumption (UGAP)","Assumptions required (aperiodic-unichain, non-degeneracy, local stability) are strictly weaker and easier to verify than those in prior work for exponential optimality","Use of a novel multivariate Lyapunov function technique to analyze and decouple the dynamics of the two-set policy","Demonstration that local stability is fundamental for exponential asymptotic optimality, via a new lower bound result"]}

methods: Two-set policy: maintains two dynamic subsets of arms, applying Optimal Local Control to one and driving the other towards optimal state distribution before merging
Novel multivariate Lyapunov function technique for analysis
problems: Achieving exponential asymptotic optimality in infinite-horizon average-reward restless bandit problems without the global attractor assumption (UGAP)
Understanding the fundamental mechanisms and assumptions required for exponential asymptotic optimality in restless bandits
metrics: Optimality gap (difference between average reward per arm under the policy and the optimal policy)

results.value: O(exp(-C N)) under aperiodic-unichain, non-degeneracy, and local stability assumptions

results.metric: Lower bound on optimality gap for unstable RBs

novelty_claims: First policy (two-set policy) to achieve exponential asymptotic optimality (O(exp(-C N)) optimality gap) in average-reward restless bandits without requiring the global attractor assumption (UGAP)
Assumptions required (aperiodic-unichain, non-degeneracy, local stability) are strictly weaker and easier to verify than those in prior work for exponential optimality
Use of a novel multivariate Lyapunov function technique to analyze and decouple the dynamics of the two-set policy
Demonstration that local stability is fundamental for exponential asymptotic optimality, via a new lower bound result

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Stochastic-Programming-based (SP-based) policy using Gaussian approximation","Construction of a Gaussian stochastic system that captures both mean and variance of RMAB dynamics","Solving a stochastic program for the Gaussian system to obtain the policy"],"problems":["Finite-horizon Restless Multi-Armed Bandit (RMAB) problem with N homogeneous arms, especially in degenerate cases","Achieving smaller optimality gap for degenerate RMABs where LP-based policies are suboptimal"],"datasets":["Synthetic degenerate RMAB example with 2 states and 2 steps (details in Section 3.3)"],"metrics":["Optimality gap (difference between policy value and optimal value)","Total optimality gap (N × per-arm gap)"],"results":[{"metric":"Optimality gap (SP-based policy, degenerate RMABs with Uniqueness Assumption)","value":"\\tilde{\\mathcal{O}}(1/N)"},{"metric":"Optimality gap (LP-based policy, degenerate RMABs)","value":"\\Theta(1/\\sqrt{N})"},{"metric":"Total optimality gap (SP-based policy, 2-state 2-step example)","value":"Remains bounded as N increases"},{"metric":"Total optimality gap (LP-based policy, 2-state 2-step example)","value":"Grows unbounded as N increases"}],"novelty_claims":["First to achieve an \\tilde{\\mathcal{O}}(1/N) optimality gap for degenerate RMABs under a uniqueness assumption, improving over the previous best known \\mathcal{O}(1/\\sqrt{N}) gap.","Introduces a Gaussian stochastic system that incorporates both mean and variance, providing a more accurate approximation than fluid (mean-only) approximations.","Demonstrates that the LP upper bound itself is not tight for degenerate RMABs, and the SP-based policy can outperform LP-based policies in these cases."]}

methods: Stochastic-Programming-based (SP-based) policy using Gaussian approximation
Construction of a Gaussian stochastic system that captures both mean and variance of RMAB dynamics
Solving a stochastic program for the Gaussian system to obtain the policy
problems: Finite-horizon Restless Multi-Armed Bandit (RMAB) problem with N homogeneous arms, especially in degenerate cases
Achieving smaller optimality gap for degenerate RMABs where LP-based policies are suboptimal
datasets: Synthetic degenerate RMAB example with 2 states and 2 steps (details in Section 3.3)
metrics: Optimality gap (difference between policy value and optimal value)

results.metric: Optimality gap (SP-based policy, degenerate RMABs with Uniqueness Assumption)

novelty_claims: First to achieve an \tilde{\mathcal{O}}(1/N) optimality gap for degenerate RMABs under a uniqueness assumption, improving over the previous best known \mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{N}) gap.
Introduces a Gaussian stochastic system that incorporates both mean and variance, providing a more accurate approximation than fluid (mean-only) approximations.
Demonstrates that the LP upper bound itself is not tight for degenerate RMABs, and the SP-based policy can outperform LP-based policies in these cases.

Validates the method on both simple (2D double integrator) and complex (car racing) scenarios, showing general applicability.

known.","Shrinking-Time-Window Policy: adaptively selects the smallest variation parameter consistent with observed demand, used when v is unknown.","Prediction Policy: uses provided predictions as estimates of mean demand, used when prediction accuracy parameter a is known and predictions are accurate.","Prediction-Error-Robust Policy (PERP): adaptively switches between using predictions and the Fixed-Time-Window Policy based on observed prediction quality, robust to unknown prediction accuracy a (when v is known)."],"problems":["Sequential newsvendor problem with unknown, nonstationary methods: Fixed-Time-Window Policy: averages a fixed number of past demand observations to estimate mean demand, used when variation parameter v is known.
Shrinking-Time-Window Policy: adaptively selects the smallest variation parameter consistent with observed demand, used when v is unknown.
Prediction Policy: uses provided predictions as estimates of mean demand, used when prediction accuracy parameter a is known and predictions are accurate.

Prediction-Error-Robust Policy (PERP): adaptively switches between using predictions and the Fixed-Time-Window Policy based on observed prediction quality, robust to unknown prediction accuracy a (when v is known).
problems: Sequential newsvendor problem with unknown, nonstationary demand distributions.
Optimal inventory control under nonstationary and unknown demand, without knowledge of the level of nonstationarity.
Incorporating generic, possibly inaccurate, predictions into the nonstationary newsvendor problem without knowing prediction accuracy.
datasets: Daily web traffic for Wikipedia.com (various languages)
Daily foot traffic across the Rossmann store chain
Daily visitors at a Japanese restaurant
metrics: Regret (expected difference in total cost between the policy and a clairvoyant optimal policy)
Optimality gap (proportion of the gap between the best and worst baseline policies closed by the proposed policy)

results.metric: Worst-case regret (Nonstationary Newsvendor, no predictions, known v)
Worst-case regret (Nonstationary Newsvendor, no predictions, unknown v)
Worst-case regret (with predictions, known v and a)
Worst-case regret (with predictions, known v, unknown a)
Worst-case regret (with predictions, unknown v and a)
Empirical cost (Rossmann upper store, no prediction)
Empirical cost (Rossmann lower store, no prediction)

novelty_claims: First policy for the nonstationary newsvendor problem that achieves order-optimal regret without requiring knowledge of the level of nonstationarity (variation parameter v).

First to introduce a model for generic predictions (with arbitrary accuracy and no statistical assumptions) in the nonstationary newsvendor problem, and to propose a policy that incorporates these predictions without knowing their accuracy.
Provides matching upper and lower bounds for regret in both the settings with and without predictions, and for both known and unknown variation/prediction accuracy parameters.
Empirically demonstrates that the proposed policy closes approximately 74% of the gap between the best approaches based on nonstationarity and predictions alone, across three real-world datasets.

superior to prior model-based and model-free methods on DMControl and Meta-World

novelty_claims: Proposes TD-MPC, a framework that jointly learns a task-oriented latent dynamics model and terminal value function via temporal difference learning for model predictive control.
Latent dynamics model is learned purely from rewards, not from state or image prediction, improving sample efficiency and focusing on task-relevant features.
Back-propagation of gradients from reward and TD-objective through multiple rollout steps to improve long-horizon predictions and reduce compounding errors.
Introduces a modality-agnostic prediction loss in latent space to enforce temporal consistency without explicit state or image prediction.
Demonstrates superior sample efficiency and asymptotic performance on both state and image-based continuous control tasks compared to prior work.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["LP-index policy: a policy constructed from the solution of a linear program, shown to be asymptotically optimal with square root convergence rate on all models and with exponential rate under certain conditions.","LP-update policy: a repeated LP-index policy that solves a new LP at each decision epoch, leveraging the current state information.","General framework for analyzing control policies for restless Markovian bandit models via deterministic maps from arm distributions to decision vectors, with randomized rounding for finite N.","Characterization of policies based on properties such as LP-compatibility, Lipschitz continuity, and local linearity around the LP solution."],"problems":["Restless Markovian bandit problem: maximizing expected total reward by activating a subset of arms at each decision epoch, under finite and infinite time horizons, with a large number of arms and a proportional activation budget.","Establishing necessary and sufficient conditions for asymptotic optimality and convergence rates (square root and exponential) of control policies for restless bandits.","Constructing policies that are asymptotically optimal and analyzing their convergence rates, especially in the presence or absence of degeneracy (non-degenerate vs. degenerate models)."],"datasets":["No real-world datasets; numerical experiments are mentioned but specific datasets are not described, as the work is theoretical and uses simulated or synthetic data for experiments."],"metrics":["Optimality gap (difference between the performance of the policy and the optimal policy)","Convergence rate of the optimality gap as a function of the number of arms N (e.g., O(1/sqrt(N)), O(1/N), e^{-O(N)})"],"results":[{"metric":"Optimality gap (asymptotic) for LP-index policy (all models)","value":"O(1/sqrt(N))"},{"metric":"Optimality gap (asymptotic) for LP-index policy (non-degenerate, finite horizon)","value":"e^{-O(N)}"},{"metric":"Optimality gap (asymptotic) for LP-update policy (non-degenerate, infinite horizon with UGAP)","value":"e^{-O(N)}"}],"novelty_claims":["Provides a new general framework to analyze the asymptotic performance and convergence rates of control policies for restless bandits, based on deterministic maps and their properties.","Establishes necessary and sufficient conditions for a policy to be asymptotically optimal, to achieve square root convergence, and to achieve exponential convergence, unifying and generalizing previous results.","Shows that the existence of an LP-compatible Lipschitz continuous policy is always guaranteed for finite horizon restless bandits, and characterizes when exponential convergence is possible (non-degeneracy).","Introduces the LP-update policy, which solves a new LP at each decision epoch, and analyzes its convergence properties.","Demonstrates that the asymptotic performance of a control policy is intimately linked to the properties of the LP relaxation, and provides a new perspective compared to previous works that focused on constructing specific policies first."]}

methods: LP-index policy: a policy constructed from the solution of a linear program, shown to be asymptotically optimal with square root convergence rate on all models and with exponential rate under certain conditions.
LP-update policy: a repeated LP-index policy that solves a new LP at each decision epoch, leveraging the current state information.
General framework for analyzing control policies for restless Markovian bandit models via deterministic maps from arm distributions to decision vectors, with randomized rounding for finite N.
Characterization of policies based on properties such as LP-compatibility, Lipschitz continuity, and local linearity around the LP solution.
problems: Restless Markovian bandit problem: maximizing expected total reward by activating a subset of arms at each decision epoch, under finite and infinite time horizons, with a large number of arms and a proportional activation budget.
Establishing necessary and sufficient conditions for asymptotic optimality and convergence rates (square root and exponential) of control policies for restless bandits.
Constructing policies that are asymptotically optimal and analyzing their convergence rates, especially in the presence or absence of degeneracy (non-degenerate vs. degenerate models).
datasets: No real-world datasets; numerical experiments are mentioned but specific datasets are not described, as the work is theoretical and uses simulated or synthetic data for experiments.
metrics: Optimality gap (difference between the performance of the policy and the optimal policy)
Convergence rate of the optimality gap as a function of the number of arms N (e.g., O(1/sqrt(N)), O(1/N), e^{-O(N)})

results.metric: Optimality gap (asymptotic) for LP-index policy (all models)
Optimality gap (asymptotic) for LP-index policy (non-degenerate, finite horizon)
Optimality gap (asymptotic) for LP-update policy (non-degenerate, infinite horizon with UGAP)

novelty_claims: Provides a new general framework to analyze the asymptotic performance and convergence rates of control policies for restless bandits, based on deterministic maps and their properties.

Establishes necessary and sufficient conditions for a policy to be asymptotically optimal, to achieve square root convergence, and to achieve exponential convergence, unifying and generalizing previous results.

Shows that the existence of an LP-compatible Lipschitz continuous policy is always guaranteed for finite horizon restless bandits, and characterizes when exponential convergence is possible (non-degeneracy).
Introduces the LP-update policy, which solves a new LP at each decision epoch, and analyzes its convergence properties.

Demonstrates that the asymptotic performance of a control policy is intimately linked to the properties of the LP relaxation, and provides a new perspective compared to previous works that focused on constructing specific policies first.
